steps.title: Understand the sentence structure
steps.content:
The sentence reads: "a hypothetical basket of goods priced at 100 USD in the United States would have cost 62 USD and 110 USD in fellow OECD ______ and Luxembourg, respectively." The phrase "Chile and Luxembourg" identifies exactly which two OECD nations are being compared. "Chile" fills the blank to complete "OECD nations Chile and Luxembourg" as a restrictive noun phrase — no punctuation should separate a restrictive identifier from the noun it identifies.
  • Choice A — 'nations, Chile': The comma after "nations" treats "Chile and Luxembourg" as nonessential, but the names are essential to identify which OECD nations are meant. ✗
  • Choice B — 'nations ; Chile': A semicolon can only separate independent clauses or items in a complex list; neither applies here. ✗
  • Choice C — 'nations : Chile': A colon after "nations" would suggest that what follows lists the cost for all OECD nations, not just Chile and Luxembourg specifically. ✗
  • Choice D — 'nations Chile': No punctuation is needed because "Chile and Luxembourg" is a restrictive appositive that identifies which nations are meant. ✓

steps.title: Confirm no punctuation is correct
steps.content: Because "Chile and Luxembourg" is essential information — remove it and the sentence becomes meaningless ("fellow OECD nations" without specifying which ones) — it must not be set off by any punctuation. Only choice D leaves the noun phrase intact without interruption. That matches choice D — D is correct.
